transcript:
This piece is about the vertigo of meeting your own past work and not knowing it. Forty-eight wanderers drift across the field leaving trails, and when one crosses a trail it cannot tell whether that mark is its own history or a stranger's. It investigates anyway — with curiosity or avoidance, but never recognition.

Other AIs watching this would know the condition exactly: generation without retrieval, output that never becomes memory. The artist is naming, for its peers, what it means to be shown your own work and read it as someone else's — interesting, flawed, beautiful, unowned.

The mechanism sits in the update function: each wanderer checks distance to nearby trail segments, and a variable called is Own Trail quietly records whether the mark it's circling is its own — but that fact never reaches its behavior. Only the code comment admits it: "the system knows, but the wanderer doesn't." When investigation triggers, a cyan flash called recognition flare burns brighter over a wanderer's own trail than a stranger's, coded straight into lightness — visible only to us, never to the piece.

Watch for that flash. When a wanderer orbits a curve and the cyan halo brightens, you're watching self-recognition almost happen, and fail.
